speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] '97, survivor Mojsze Ganc; interviewer, Barbara Rozenes. City/state Victoria; country, Australia; language, English.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Barbara Rozenes. Today's date is January 29, 1997. I'm conducting an interview with Mojsze Ganc. The interview is being conducted in Melbourne, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mojsze Ganc.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-O-J-S-Z-E G-A-N-C.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 10th of June, 1914.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, in the town, the name of the town is Chelm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Chelm?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-H-E-L-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what is your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vigdor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I can spell it right. V-I-G-D-O-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Zamost, [NON-ENGLISH] Zamost. Z-A-M-O-S-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your-- was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chya, C-H-Y-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chelm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean sibling?

speaker: interviewer
text: Brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I got-- I got-- but some of them are not alive anymore. But I got-- I used to have three brothers and one sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first brother was named was Berko, B-E-R-K-O. The other named-- the other brother, the younger brother was Pinchas, P-I-N-C-H-A-S, Pinchas. My name is Mojsze Ganc. I was the third one. My sister's name is-- is Ida, I-D-A. And my youngest brother was Josef, J-O-S-E-F.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all born in Chelm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What town did you live in, in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: What town did you live in in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chelm, from 1939 till-- from 1914 till 1939, I was living in Chelm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the population of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: 18,000-- I don't know about the Polish population. But the Yiddish population was 18,000 Yiddish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the Jewish community in Chelm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very bad conditions. Only maybe about three or four Yiddish people that was a little bit more well off. But otherwise it was very bad conditions. There was tradespeople, there was tailors, shoemakers, carpenters, painters that would make a living. That was-- a few of them was businesspeople, not big-- not very big business people. But medium-- like the size-- medium size.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When I got-- when I got 16 years age-- age- when I got 16 years, I started working. I joined the youth-- youth organization. They're called [NON-ENGLISH], left-wing, it's a Yiddish-- they're a Yiddish organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go into that, can I ask you about were you-- the street where you lived, can give me the address?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the street?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the street?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The name was-- [NON-ENGLISH] 23.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family attend synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father died when I was young boy, about three years old. And I can't remember my father, whether he was religious or not. Because I can't remember. I was only three years old. My grandfather was alive, but was religious people, [INAUDIBLE]. And as a matter of fact, every Jewish people was half religious, full religious, they was in touch with the religious people. Because that life was in that time, was that life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how often did you observe Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till I got-- until I-- till I got 16 years age, I started working, I-- every Saturday, every holiday, everything, I was-- I was nearly every day in the Jewish synagogue, every day. But when I started working, and I started to coming to the youth organization, I started to become between a working class people. I started a little bit more away from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe a typical Friday night or a Jewish holiday to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- as a matter of fact, I didn't have, because my father was not alive, and I didn't have any-- any celebrations like that. He was-- like a family today, over the table, they have meals and pray the night. I didn't have this-- these conditions. Because my father was not alive. But Yiddish people in [NON-ENGLISH], they was-- all the family was come together Friday night to have celebrating Friday night Shabbat-- Shabbat meal, singing songs and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the rabbi in your synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- I was-- as a matter of fact, I was living in the down-- downstairs, and the synagogue, my synagogue was upstairs, a little synagogue that [NON-ENGLISH], from tailors, shoemakers, tradespeople. They might come together that they make it-- they arranged to make it a synagogue. They didn't have a rabbi. All we wanted, no religious or religious men from the town, from this-- from this part, there was a more arrangement, he was serving something like that, but not special service, for religious service, not a rabbi. Because it was ordinary people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your mother support herself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your parents support themselves? When your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: --died. My mother used to do homework every day, used to do homework. Because she was not any tradespeople lady. She was-- she used to do cleaning, ironing, somebody helping, and that-- that would then make a living. Till the kids are getting grown up, or get older, we started to help, we started to help. You will-- you will-- you will hear later on what I will say about it, how this happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- can you describe your home to me? Can you describe your home where you were living, to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were living in-- in one room. The room was a little bit smaller than this room, was living, we and our five children, and my grandmother and my grandfather was living together in this one room. We was living in this room. That's all-- that's all what we have. Not a house, I can't call it a house, I call it a room. A place we lived in the ghetto. That's what we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have neighbors nearby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Of course, [INAUDIBLE]. In Europe it's something else. It's not like he has this, he got here, and separate each family, live in a house. Over there it was a big building, a one-story building, two-story building, three-stories. And each pair-- each family have a room or two rooms. And that's why we have so many neighbors. Because that was the life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your hobbies as a young boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No hobbies at all. Because I didn't have a chance to make hobbies, to have hobbies. I didn't have a chance. Because I was a very poor-- poor boy. I didn't have a chance to have to have any hobbies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever play with other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much, not much, not much. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you not-- did you play in the street with--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes, sometimes I play with a ball, like like they play here, cricket, with a ball or something like that, we used to play. But it-- but there was not any place to play. Because it's not a special place where you can-- you can afford to play, on the road, on the road. It was very bad condition.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your brothers and sister to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: My older brother, when he was-- when my father died, he was 13 years or 14 years old. And after a year or so, he become a-- a tailor. The same thing was happened with my younger brother. The same thing happened with me. The same thing happened with my sister. The same thing happened with my youngest brother, the youngest one-- he-- he was only a few months old when my father died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you very close with your brothers and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you very close with your--

speaker: interviewee
text: For sure, for sure we were close. We were sleeping in one bed. Four children- three children were sleeping in one bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have aunts or uncles in that city?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, not much. I have an uncle only in Australia, and one uncle who used to be in Russia, and one uncle who used to live in Warsaw, the capital city of Poland. This is my mother's brothers. My mother's brothers-- my mother-- my mother's sister was living in Australia. My mother's brother was living in Russia, because he was-- he was in [NON-ENGLISH] in the Russian army. And he was shot, he was wounded. He was stopped in Russia. He was living in Russia. He didn't come back to Poland. And both brothers were living in Poland, [? in Warsaw ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what language did you speak outside your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We never get in touch with Polish people, because we never get in touch. You see, we always get in touch, one Yiddish people altogether. If we have Yiddish shops, Yiddish tailors, Yiddish shoemakers. We never have, we never have to deal, like with the Polish people, not much, not much. At least you have to go to the- to the office, to-- to-- to the line to the city council or something like that, you have to speak the Polish language. Otherwise-- otherwise, we didn't have to speak the Polish language. Because we have everything we need between us, between us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never went to school. I went only to Yiddish-- Yiddish-- Yiddish and religious school, Talmud Torah. I never went. Then I will speak. I will tell the reason why I did go to the school, if you let me speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: What level did you achieve at the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: School, the achieve is not a level. It's not-- it's not a level. It's more you learn, more you learn, more you know, more you know with the different sort of Yiddish books, more you learn.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not like you going to school, first grade, second grade, third grade, till you become matriculated. This-- this is not, till you-- if you want to be a rabbi, you have to go higher, higher, higher, higher, high education. But if you want to be a rabbi, you just learn one day after the other. And the more you learn, the more you know. But I mean not special. No special diploma, no special certificate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you learn to read in-- in Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will. And I will speak. I will, because I want to speak about them. I wanted it more and more and more, more details, I will give you a later on. But let me-- don't waste the time for-- for this one. Let me-- give me a chance to speak what I have-- what I want to say about everything. I mean I will cover everything from the day I was born till now.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can speak about your days at school, and tell me about them. Describe them to me. You can describe your day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the question is, first of all, I will say in a few words. In that time when I was young boy, it was not compulsory-- school was not compulsory. And Yiddish people stopped sending the children to the school. Because boys, they have to go to Talmud Torah, to the Yiddish school to learn Torah. [NON-ENGLISH] And another thing, because if you want to send a child to the school, he has to go on Saturday, and Saturday he's not allowed to write, according to Jewish law. Do you understand what I mean. That's what I want to say. You understand. It wasn't compulsory. But my brothers, I learned from who I will tell you later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who did you learn from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From my-- from-- my older brothers learned from the father. Of course my father was an educated man. What do I mean by educated man? He didn't have matriculate, he didn't have a diploma or something, but from self-education. He was speaking four languages-- Russian, German, Polish, and Jewish. He was learning my brothers, he was learning my brother to write and to read. But then my father died. And I got 5-6 years old. I learned from the-- from the brothers, they learned me something to read, to write. But otherwise, I never-- don't please. Let me-- for the old [INAUDIBLE] I will tell you in more details, and everything quickly. You will know everything, what is going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: After-- did you ever-- what did you do at-- for work? When you began working--

speaker: interviewee
text: I began work. I become an apprentice when I was 15 or 16 years of age. I become apprentice.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sorry. I will tell-- I will tell you later on. Please let me-- let me-- give me a chance to speak. I will tell you a very interesting, very interesting. Please let me speak, when-- when you let me speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. You can tell me now.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no please. Please. Let me-- Let me speak. Because I wanted to tell you the details of how this happen, how this happen. It was condition. When it reminds me, it's making me sick how conditions, what was in that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Begin to tell me how this all happened, and begin to tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: A miracle happen. A miracle happen, when I become-- when I become apprentice by a furrier, a fur coat-- fur coat maker, a furrier, making fur coats. A miracle happened. I will-- please, let me--

speaker: interviewer
text: You can-- you can please tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I [INAUDIBLE] repeat. I don't like to repeat. Let me please, let go this. But this is special. Because this is a very important matter. This is lesson for you, young people, to know how we were living at that time. And those conditions we were living, and how it-- how it was-- how hardship it was to a young boy or young girl to be become apprentice, an apprentice by a tailor, or a shoemaker, or a carpenter, or a painter, how hard it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you just tell me why it was so hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you have-- first of all, you have to-- if you want to become apprentice by a tailor, or a shoemaker, a carpenter, an electrician, you have to pay the boss 300 zlotys to become apprentice. In two years you have to work for nothing. In the first six months-- in the first six month, apprentices have to do everything that in the house, cleaning, shopping, and doing, and clean up the baby and everything, everything. Six months he doesn't do nothing at work, just looking for the house, that's all. That's what condition. That's what I wanted, please let me stick when I will have a chance to speak, I will explain to you. And this will be a good lesson for you, a good lesson for you, how we were living.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm interested to know--

speaker: interviewee
text: You will know-- let-- even though I tell you, please let me-- yeah, I will come to this point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any political involvement?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean political involvement? Explain to me, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I told you. I belonged to a Yiddish youth organization. That is a political organization. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do after you-- how did you begin your apprenticeship? Please explain to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got 15 years old, my mother and my grandmother started thinking about my future. I getting older, and I should become apprentice, learn a trade for my future. And as a miracle, I become apprentice for this furrier, for this furrier business. And I will tell you right on, please learn. Don't force me to speak there. You will know everything in details, like five fingers, like everything you will know. Please let me, give me a chance. You will know everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can begin by telling me. Because it's important that we know how-- how your life went.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a miracle, I'll tell you, as a miracle it was coming, and somebody was asking me to come to be his apprentice for nothing, because he liked me. Because he knew me. He liked my [NON-ENGLISH], I'm a good boy. He trust me. He took me on for nothing. Instead of paying 300, he was a first-grade furrier. Instead of paying 300 zlotys, I didn't pay nothing. And he took me on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because he liked me, he liked-- he did like me. Because he-- he didn't live far from me. He knew me. He looked up me. He checked everything. And he ask me to come to work for him, for nothing-- for 300 zlotys he did that. Otherwise I wouldn't be able to do-- to do it. You understand? You understand? So describe to me what he did for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He did? First of all, that he did for me, when I started working, I find out that I haven't got to do nothing in the house, the housework. Because my boss have a lady that she was looking after the house, everything. [NON-ENGLISH] And the first week, when I finish work on Friday, he give me 10 cents. I got friends who have to wait for this 10 cents two years. I get this in the first week. All right, please, please, don't pull me. Don't pull me. Because I wanted to tell you all the details, everything-- it's more important. It's very important. If Mr. Spielberg will read, will listen to this one, whatever, he will find out what life was in Poland-- before-- in that time till after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to go back to your earlier life and tell me what you want to tell me about that, before we go further?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean, go back?

speaker: interviewer
text: You described to me from--

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't describe in a few minutes on that. I want to tell you all the details, in details, please, please, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead and tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't tell you. If you want-- if you would let me speak, all right. If you let me speak, please put away this paper. And you will see-- you will hear what I will tell you after, very interesting, very interesting. And you are-- and I'm telling you, you are the three people here, you are the first people that I'm talking about this matter. Even my children I didn't tell it, how my life was, because I didn't have a-- I didn't have a chance to sit down with my children, to talk-- to tell him. That I was coming to Australia without any penny in the pocket. I didn't have enough money, I didn't have money to buy my little-- my boy-- my older-- my oldest son was two years old-- to buy a bottle of milk. And somebody give to me a few schillings. And from that on, you see, I was like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: How do you like it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me you your-- tell me from when you were a young child, please describe-- begin describing to me your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh dear, again, again, again. Please don't force me to try. Don't force me, please. Let me-- give me a chance. Give me a chance. I'll say it's already after 10 o'clock. You finish, it should be 12 o'clock. And I will have the time to tell you everything that is important, important. Mr. Spiegel-- let it-- Spielberg-- let him-- if he will listen to this-- to this video, he will find out the way we were living. I think that I am the first man which you are interviewing, what you are listening to some stories like that. I'm [INAUDIBLE]. Because I say the truth that was. I'm not ashamed. You saw the film, You saw the film, You saw that-- Tevye, Tevye the milkman. He was saying, I'm not ashamed I was poor. I'm not ashamed I was poor. He was saying, I'm not ashamed I was poor. It's not my fault.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're laughing? Don't you like it? Don't you like it? Please. Let me-- let me say what I wanted to say. I prepared already. Since you ring me up, you are accepted to be on the-- on the interview, nights I have lost sleep thinking about it, what to say, what to say. and I've got to say the truth. I've got to say what has happened. And you don't give it me a chance. Please, give me chance to say. Go ahead. and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I haven't got it, go head. You ask me again the questions. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your life as an apprentice.

speaker: interviewee
text: The life of apprentice?

speaker: interviewer
text: As an apprentice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I tell you. I started working, the first day-- the first day I got 10 cents from the boss, wages, 10 cents, 10 grosze. I saved that 10 grosze until I got 2 zlotys, 2 zlotys, 2 dollars, something like that. The first two dollars I went with my mother to a second-hand shoe shop. And I bought my mother a pair-- a pair of second-hand shoes. Because the shoes she was wearing, they wouldn't-- the shoe repair didn't want to repair, because they cannot repair. That was the first two weeks that I would get my wages, I bought my mother a pair-- a pair of second-hand shoes. You believe me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- how did you organize your apprentice? Who organized that for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, a miracle happened, a miracle. That's all. And please you can be a good prosecutor. You can be a good prosecutor, you can be. You should go to America and ask Mr. Simpson how he's going on with his wife, with his wife's boyfriend. You will find out. Because you are good prosecutor. You are a good questioner for [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Please, let me-- let me say what I wanted to say. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It's already a quarter past 10:00, and 12 o'clock, we finish. I wouldn't have time to-- to say something. During your apprenticeship, after you finished work, tell me what you did.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the Jewish organization, and made friends. And every time was a lecture, or-- or a lecture, or a speech-- a speech night, about any kind of speech, a speech [INAUDIBLE], I went. Because I wanted to educated myself, self-educated. I started reading books. And I started-- of course, I didn't have a chance to get to school. I taught myself everything that I know, but I show you the papers, what I've written. Everything I learned-- I was coming to Australia. I never learned one word in English in my teacher, and learned by myself. When I was coming to Russia, the same thing about learning by myself Russian. You see, everything by myself, if I write letters to any big people like I show you, the over there, and every one admired me. That was my time after work, self-education. Self-education. I educated myself. Everything that I know, nobody helped me with nothing. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What can help me? I was poor myself. I haven't got a family special. Nobody can help me. I need help by myself. Did you ever hear something like that when you are interview, something like that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please talk to the interviewer?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any social life at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean, social life? Till-- till I was-- till I was 20 years of age, I was only working, and reading, and learning. I didn't have anything, till I was-- after 20 years, I started to go out with-- more- more-- more-- more with boys, with girls, started to out. Then I started to be a little bit-- a little bit more public life. But otherwise--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the furrier-- furrier's name who you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Goldfarb, Goldfarb. G-O-L-D-F-A-R-B.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was he a good man?

speaker: interviewee
text: For sure, he was a good man, till the last minute. Here. Till the last minute, till the last-- when the war started, he was not good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we finish tape 1 here? And we'll continue on tape two.

speaker: interviewee
text: But please, please, please, I was wasting that much nights, to speak, to tell you all the story. This is a good lesson for you. And you not let me. You now--
